data: Gabriele Lancillotto Castelli principe di Torremuzza, Le antiche iscrizioni di Palermo (Palermo, 1762), http://arachne.uni-koeln.de/books/Castelli1762
Bilingual sign advertising engraving of inscriptions

The text fills the face of the plaque, divided vertically by a deeply incised line into two unequal halves, with a column of Greek on the left (wider) and Latin on the right). Each column is approximately centred, and consists of seven lines of text, with the first two lines notably larger than the following five.
Greek text includes lunate sigma and epsilon; extended apex to delta and alpha. Latin text is is regular with serifs, open P, closed R. There are no word dividers or punctuation in either text.

Augustan or Julio-Claudian,
                                by the letter forms; also, archaic spelling of some of the Latin, with other Sicilian
                                parallels, rules out much later date (so Wilson 1990); Manni Piraino thinks later 2nd century CE

Fragment of an architrave dedicated to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus
No details of the form of the text preserved, although Torremuzza describes as grand letters in a single line
The nomenclature in this precise form only apparently attested for Caracalla, but in principle several of those from Marcus Aurelius to Elagabalus are possible

Fragment of an inscription referencing one of the emperors styled Marcus Aurelius
Tall and very narrow letters, fairly crudely carved, with AV in ligature.
The reference could be to one of several emperors from Marcus Aurelius (161 CE) onwards, although a third century date is more likely given the form of the letters.
fragment of an inscription referencing one of the emperors styled marcus aurelius
1Imp(eratori?) · Caes(ari?) · M(arco?) · A͡uṛẹ[lio? ---]

The text is laid out to present names in columns, separated off horizontally into separate sections for different magistrates or priests. There is a use of vacats both horizontally, between sections, and vertically seemingly separating names from patronyms.

Very neat, regular small letters of fairly square module. Omicon and omega of more or less equal size to other letters. Alpha with straight bar; epsilon with short middle bar; Omega circular and open. Pronounced terminal serifs visible on some letters, e.g. epsilon and tau.

A tessera hospitalis recording xenia between Imulch and Lyson
Four lines of Greek letters, with a consistent left margin, starting at the top of the rear (smooth) side of the tessera, with small vacat below.

Small, slightly irregular letters without serifs. Lunate epsilon, sigma and omega; mu formed by four, equal slanting strokes; omicron generally slightly smaller than other letters; beta is closed, but with separated loops; rho is closed; xsi in form of a Z with horizontal cross bar; kappa has short equal length arms.
2nd century BCE, or possibly 1st century BCE (the letter forms look more plausibly second than 1st century)

Epitaph for Domitius Himeraeus, his sister Domitia and his wife Alfia Zotica
Three lines of roughly equal and centred latin letters filling the face of the stone, words separated by lightly carved interpuncts.
Simply cut reasonably regular letters, with one instance of ligature and an extended T in line 2.
Bivona suggests that the onomastics (presence of tribal, use of Aulus and lack of cognomen in the sister's name) imply a date in the first half of the C1 CE

Torremuzza is explicit that the text includes a quadrate lunate sigma and square omicron.
Appears to have the distinctive Sicilian formula of 'chrestos kai amemptos', age in years and a family dedicant, indicating Roman imperial period.

The text is set out over two lines on the lower part fo the front face of the slabe, in a field (4 cm high) demarcated from the upper part of the face by an incised horizontal line.

Small neat early Hellenistic letters, Alpha with straight bar, epsilon with short middle bar, zeta formed by vertical with horizontal bars top and bottom; Mu with four sloping strokes, full length; omicron, omega and theta all smaller than other letters; sigma with four equal strokes, top and bottom angled outwards; omega open with short horizontal tails.

Torremuzza indicates that the gaps in the lines and at line ends are uncertain in size, as stone is missing between the fragments.
Distinguished by the presence of rhomboid omicron and theta (on which Torremuzza discourses, 1762, p.243).
later Hellenistic or imperial, by the lettering

Slightly unevenly laid out over 6 lines (Castelli provides an engraving, to emphasise that the presentation of Muratori is misleading)

Somewhat irregular and variable letters, thinly cut without serifs. alpha has broken bar; beta two approximately even, usually closed eyes; epsilon straight and bars of equal lengths; zeta has curving lower stroke; kappa has full length arms; mu has mid-strokes that start and finish below the top of the verticals and only descend partway; nu is reversed in line 6; rho is both angular and circular; sigma is lunate; omega is lunate.
